content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format

1→# AI Daemon
     2→
     3→A lightweight AI framework built on Spring Boot 4 and Spring AI. Connect any LLM provider, extend capabilities with skills and MCPs, schedule autonomous jobs, and interact through REST, Web UI, or terminal -- all from a single self-hosted service.
     4→
     5→![Example use case](images/example.png)
     6→
     7→## Architecture
     8→
     9→```mermaid
    10→graph TB
    11→    User([User])
    12→
    13→    subgraph Interface["Interface Layer"]
    14→        REST[REST API]
    15→        WebUI[Web UI]
    16→        CLI[Interactive CLI]
    17→    end
    18→
    19→    User -->|HTTP / curl| REST
    20→    User -->|Browser| WebUI
    21→    User -->|Terminal| CLI
    22→    WebUI --> REST
    23→
    24→    subgraph Engine["AI Engine"]
    25→        Chat[Chat Service]
    26→        Conv[Conversation Manager]
    27→        Providers[Provider Registry]
    28→        Memory[Persistent Memory]
    29→        Skills[Skills Reader]
    30→        Shell[Shell Executor]
    31→        Scheduler[Job Scheduler]
    32→        MCPTools[MCP Tools]
    33→    end
    34→
    35→    subgraph Providers_Sub["LLM Providers"]
    36→        OpenAI[OpenAI]
    37→        Anthropic[Anthropic]
    38→        Gemini[Gemini]
    39→        Ollama[Ollama / Local]
    40→    end
    41→
    42→    subgraph External["External Integrations"]
    43→        MCPRemote[Remote MCPs]
    44→        MCPLocal[Local MCPs - stdio]
    45→        Smithery[Smithery Skills Registry]
    46→    end
    47→
    48→    subgraph Storage["~/.aidaemon/"]
    49→        ProvidersJSON[providers.json]
    50→        MemoryJSON[memory.json]
    51→        JobsJSON[jobs.json]
    52→        ConversationsDir[conversations/]
    53→        SkillsDir[skills/]
    54→        McpsDir[mcps/]
    55→    end
    56→
    57→    REST --> Chat
    58→    REST --> Conv
    59→    CLI --> Conv
    60→
    61→    Conv -->|multi-turn history| Chat
    62→    Chat --> Providers
    63→    Providers --> OpenAI & Anthropic & Gemini & Ollama
    64→
    65→    Chat -->|tool calls| Memory & Skills & Shell & Scheduler & MCPTools
    66→
    67→    Memory -->|read/write| MemoryJSON
    68→    Skills -->|read| SkillsDir
    69→    Scheduler -->|persist| JobsJSON
    70→    Scheduler -->|executes via| Chat
    71→    Conv -->|persist| ConversationsDir
    72→    MCPTools --> MCPRemote & MCPLocal
    73→    MCPRemote & MCPLocal -.->|config| McpsDir
    74→    Smithery -->|install all files| SkillsDir
    75→    Providers -.->|persist| ProvidersJSON
    76→
    77→    Shell -->|guarded by flag| OS[Host OS]
    78→
    79→    style User fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    80→    style Storage fill:#c9a800,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    81→    style External fill:#1a7a7a,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    82→```
    83→
    84→### Delegation
    85→
    86→When delegation is enabled, the main agent can spawn sub-agents to handle subtasks in parallel. Sub-agents run asynchronously; when they finish, the parent is woken with a status update and can synthesize results or send revision work.
    87→Sub-agents can create their own subagents. The diagram shows one parent and only top subagents for simplicitly.
    88→
    89→```mermaid
    90→sequenceDiagram
    91→    participant User
    92→    participant Parent as Parent Agent
    93→    participant DelegationService
    94→    participant Sub1 as Sub-agent 1
    95→    participant Sub2 as Sub-agent 2
    96→
    97→    User->>Parent: Message
    98→    Parent->>Parent: Estimates time > threshold
    99→    Parent->>DelegationService: delegateToSubAgent x2
   100→    DelegationService->>Sub1: Create and run sub-conversation
   101→    DelegationService->>Sub2: Create and run sub-conversation
   102→    Parent->>User: Delegated, will respond when ready
   103→    Sub1->>DelegationService: Completed
   104→    Sub2->>DelegationService: Completed
   105→    DelegationService->>Parent: Delegation Status Update with sub results
   106→    Parent->>Parent: Review, addWorkToSubAgent or synthesize
   107→    Parent->>User: Final response
   108→```
   109→
   110→- **Tools:** `delegateToSubAgent(name, instruction)` creates a sub-conversation and queues it. `addWorkToSubAgent(subConversationId, instruction)` sends follow-up work to a sub-agent after reviewing its output.
   111→- **Config:** `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`, `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds=30`. The model is instructed to delegate when its estimated time exceeds the threshold.
   112→
   113→## Features
   114→
   115→- **Multi-provider** -- OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama. Add/remove providers at runtime with API key persistence.
   116→- **Conversations** -- Stateful multi-turn sessions with full chat history, persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. The provider (agent) is selected **per prompt**: set or change it for the conversation so the next message uses that provider. Conversations have creation timestamps and can be sorted by time.
   117→- **Streaming** -- Conversation messages can be sent with `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` (SSE). Chunks include `reasoning`, `answer`, and `tool` types so the UI can show thinking and tools in real time.
   118→- **Delegation** -- Optional sub-agent delegation: the AI splits work into sub-conversations that run in parallel; when they complete, the parent is notified and can synthesize or request revisions. Enable with `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`.
   119→- **Thinking / reasoning** -- Supported for providers that expose it (e.g. Anthropic extended thinking). Reasoning is streamed separately and shown in the Web UI; scheduled job results include thinking in the stored output.
   120→- **Prompt caching** -- Conversation-history and prompt caching are used where supported (e.g. Anthropic, Gemini) to reduce cost and latency.
   121→- **Context window** -- Optional `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` (character limit) trims older messages and, for named bots, personal memory so the prompt fits. Use `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` (0–1) to split the budget between conversation history and bot personal memory. The AI can use the `retrieveOlderMessages` tool to fetch older conversation content when context is trimmed.
   122→- **Persistent memory** -- AI can save and recall information across sessions via `memory.json`.
   123→- **Skills** -- Drop instruction files into `~/.aidaemon/skills/` or install from [Smithery](https://smithery.ai) via REST endpoint. The AI reads them for domain-specific context.
   124→- **MCP support** -- Connect remote (Streamable HTTP, SSE) and local (stdio) MCP servers. Drop JSON configs into `~/.aidaemon/mcps/` and reload, or add [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCPs via chat (Notion, Google Calendar, etc.).
   125→- **Scheduled jobs** -- AI creates cron jobs (recurring or one-time) that autonomously execute instructions on schedule. AI can also list and cancel them.
   126→- **Shell access** -- Optionally allow the AI to execute host commands (git, docker, curl, etc.). Controlled by a global toggle endpoint.
   127→- **Web UI** -- React app in `frontend/webui/` for providers, conversations, and streaming chat with reasoning and tool visibility.
   128→- **Interactive CLI** -- Terminal-based chat with provider selection, conversation management, and shell toggle.
   129→
   130→## Prerequisites
   131→
   132→- Java 25+
   133→- Maven (or use the included `mvnw` wrapper)
   134→
   135→## Quick Start
   136→
   137→```bash
   138→# Clone and build
   139→git clone <repo-url>
   140→cd aidaemon
   141→./mvnw spring-boot:run
   142→```
   143→
   144→On Windows PowerShell:
   145→```powershell
   146→.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run
   147→```
   148→
   149→The server starts on `http://localhost:8080`.
   150→
   151→### 1. Register a Provider
   152→
   153→```bash
   154→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
   155→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   156→  -d '{"name":"my-gpt","type":"OPENAI","apiKey":"...","model":"gpt-4o"}'
   157→```
   158→
   159→Supported types: `OPENAI`, `ANTHROPIC`, `GEMINI`, `OLLAMA`
   160→
   161→For Ollama (no API key needed):
   162→```bash
   163→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
   164→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   165→  -d '{"name":"local-llama","type":"OLLAMA","baseUrl":"http://localhost:11434","model":"llama3"}'
   166→```
   167→
   168→### 2. Chat (Stateless)
   169→
   170→```bash
   171→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/chat/{providerId} \
   172→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   173→  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}]}'
   174→```
   175→
   176→### 3. Conversations (Stateful)
   177→
   178→Conversations are persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. Each has an optional name. The provider is selected per prompt: set it when creating or via `PATCH`; the next message you send will use that provider.
   179→
   180→```bash
   181→# Create a conversation
   182→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations \
   183→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   184→  -d '{"name":"My chat","providerId":"<id>"}'
   185→
   186→# Send messages (history is maintained server-side)
   187→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages \
   188→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   189→  -d '{"message":"What did I just ask you?"}'
   190→
   191→# Stream response (SSE: reasoning, answer, tool chunks)
   192→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream \
   193→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   194→  -d '{"message":"Explain X"}' \
   195→  -N
   196→
   197→# Set which provider to use for the next prompt(s)
   198→curl -X PATCH http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id} \
   199→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   200→  -d '{"providerId":"other-provider-id"}'
   201→```
   202→
   203→### 4. Interactive CLI
   204→
   205→```bash
   206→./mvnw spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli
   207→```
   208→
   209→On Windows PowerShell:
   210→```powershell
   211→.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run "-Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli"
   212→```
   213→
   214→Commands inside CLI: `/quit`, `/new` (new conversation), `/shell` (toggle shell access).
   215→
   216→### 5. Web UI
   217→
   218→From the project root, build and run the React UI (proxy to the backend):
   219→
   220→```bash
   221→cd frontend/webui
   222→npm install
   223→npm run dev
   224→```
   225→
   226→Connect to the URL shown (e.g. Vite dev server). Configure providers and conversations, then chat; responses stream with reasoning and tool calls when available.
   227→
   228→## Skills
   229→
   230→Skills are folders containing a `SKILL.md` and optional resources (scripts, references, assets) that give the AI domain-specific knowledge.
   231→
   232→**Manual install:** Drop a skill folder into `~/.aidaemon/skills/`. With Smithery MCP enabled, ask the AI to search and install skills (e.g. "install the skill-creator skill from Smithery"); it uses `searchSmitherySkills` and `installSmitherySkill`.
   233→
   234→**Manage skills:** Ask the AI to list skills (`listSkills`), list or read files in a skill (`listSkillFiles`, `readSkillFile`), or remove a skill (`removeSkill`).
   235→
   236→## MCP Servers
   237→
   238→Create JSON config files in `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`.
   239→
   240→**Remote (Streamable HTTP):**
   241→```json
   242→{
   243→  "name": "github",
   244→  "type": "REMOTE",
   245→  "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/",
   246→  "headers": {
   247→    "Authorization": "Bearer <token>"
   248→  }
   249→}
   250→```
   251→
   252→**Remote (Legacy SSE):**
   253→```json
   254→{
   255→  "name": "my-sse-server",
   256→  "type": "SSE",
   257→  "url": "https://example.com/mcp/sse"
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→**Local (stdio):**
   262→```json
   263→{
   264→  "name": "aviation",
   265→  "type": "LOCAL",
   266→  "command": "npx",
   267→  "args": ["-y", "aviationstack-mcp-server"],
   268→  "env": {
   269→    "AVIATIONSTACK_API_KEY": "<key>"
   270→  }
   271→}
   272→```
   273→
   274→### Adding MCP via Smithery
   275→
   276→You can connect [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCP servers (e.g. Notion, Google Calendar) without writing config files by hand.
   277→
   278→1. **Enable and configure Smithery MCP** in `application.yaml` or environment:
   279→
   280→   ```yaml
   281→   aidaemon:
   282→     smithery-mcp:
   283→       enabled: true
   284→       namespace: <your-smithery-namespace>
   285→       api-key: <your-smithery-api-key>
   286→   ```
   287→
   288→   Get your namespace and API key from the [Smithery dashboard](https://smithery.ai).
   289→
   290→2. **Find a server** on [smithery.ai/servers](https://smithery.ai/servers) and note its slug (e.g. `notion`, `googlecalendar`) or full URL (e.g. `https://smithery.ai/servers/notion`).
   291→
   292→3. **Add the MCP via chat:** In any conversation, ask the AI to add the server by slug or URL, for example:
   293→   - “Add the Notion MCP from Smithery”
   294→   - “Add Smithery MCP server googlecalendar”
   295→
   296→   The AI uses the `addSmitheryMcp` tool, writes the config to `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`, and connects the server.
   297→
   298→4. **OAuth:** If the server requires sign-in, the tool returns an authorization URL. Open it in your browser, complete sign-in, then reload MCPs:
   299→
   300→   ```bash
   301→   curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   302→   ```
   303→
   304→```bash
   305→# List connected MCPs
   306→curl http://localhost:8080/api/mcps
   307→
   308→# Reload after adding/editing config files
   309→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   310→```
   311→
   312→## Shell Access
   313→
   314→Shell access is **disabled by default**. Toggle it at runtime:
   315→
   316→```bash
   317→# Enable
   318→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/enable
   319→
   320→# Disable
   321→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/disable
   322→
   323→# Check status
   324→curl http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access
   325→```
   326→
   327→When enabled, the AI can execute arbitrary commands on the host system.
   328→
   329→## Scheduled Jobs
   330→
   331→The AI can create cron jobs via tool calls. You can also manage them through REST:
   332→
   333→```bash
   334→# List all jobs
   335→curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs
   336→
   337→# View execution results
   338→curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/results
   339→
   340→# Cancel a job
   341→curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/{id}
   342→```
   343→
   344→## API Reference
   345→
   346→| Method | Endpoint | Description |
   347→|--------|----------|-------------|
   348→| `GET` | `/api/providers` | List registered providers |
   349→| `POST` | `/api/providers` | Register a new provider |
   350→| `DELETE` | `/api/providers/{id}` | Remove a provider |
   351→| `POST` | `/api/chat/{providerId}` | Stateless chat |
   352→| `POST` | `/api/conversations` | Create a conversation |
   353→| `PATCH` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Set provider for next prompt(s) (e.g. providerId) |
   354→| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages` | Send a message |
   355→| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` | Send a message (SSE stream) |
   356→| `GET` | `/api/conversations` | List conversations |
   357→| `DELETE` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Delete a conversation |
   358→| `GET` | `/api/mcps` | List connected MCP servers |
   359→| `POST` | `/api/mcps/reload` | Reload MCP configurations |
   360→| `GET` | `/api/shell-access` | Shell access status |
   361→| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/enable` | Enable shell access |
   362→| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/disable` | Disable shell access |
   363→| `GET` | `/api/jobs` | List scheduled jobs |
   364→| `GET` | `/api/jobs/results` | View job execution results |
   365→| `DELETE` | `/api/jobs/{id}` | Cancel a job |
   366→
   367→## Data Directory
   368→
   369→All persistent data lives in `~/.aidaemon/`:
   370→
   371→```
   372→~/.aidaemon/
   373→├── providers.json     # Registered LLM providers and API keys
   374→├── memory.json        # AI's persistent memory
   375→├── jobs.json          # Scheduled job definitions
   376→├── conversations/     # Conversation JSON files (stateful chats)
   377→├── skills/            # Skill folders (SKILL.md + resources)
   378→└── mcps/              # MCP server config JSON files
   379→```
   380→
   381→## Configuration
   382→
   383→Optional `application.yaml` / env properties:
   384→

file.content:
# AI Daemon

A lightweight AI framework built on Spring Boot 4 and Spring AI. Connect any LLM provider, extend capabilities with skills and MCPs, schedule autonomous jobs, and interact through REST, Web UI, or terminal -- all from a single self-hosted service.

![Example use case](images/example.png)

## Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    User([User])

    subgraph Interface["Interface Layer"]
        REST[REST API]
        WebUI[Web UI]
        CLI[Interactive CLI]
    end

    User -->|HTTP / curl| REST
    User -->|Browser| WebUI
    User -->|Terminal| CLI
    WebUI --> REST

    subgraph Engine["AI Engine"]
        Chat[Chat Service]
        Conv[Conversation Manager]
        Providers[Provider Registry]
        Memory[Persistent Memory]
        Skills[Skills Reader]
        Shell[Shell Executor]
        Scheduler[Job Scheduler]
        MCPTools[MCP Tools]
    end

    subgraph Providers_Sub["LLM Providers"]
        OpenAI[OpenAI]
        Anthropic[Anthropic]
        Gemini[Gemini]
        Ollama[Ollama / Local]
    end

    subgraph External["External Integrations"]
        MCPRemote[Remote MCPs]
        MCPLocal[Local MCPs - stdio]
        Smithery[Smithery Skills Registry]
    end

    subgraph Storage["~/.aidaemon/"]
        ProvidersJSON[providers.json]
        MemoryJSON[memory.json]
        JobsJSON[jobs.json]
        ConversationsDir[conversations/]
        SkillsDir[skills/]
        McpsDir[mcps/]
    end

    REST --> Chat
    REST --> Conv
    CLI --> Conv

    Conv -->|multi-turn history| Chat
    Chat --> Providers
    Providers --> OpenAI & Anthropic & Gemini & Ollama

    Chat -->|tool calls| Memory & Skills & Shell & Scheduler & MCPTools

    Memory -->|read/write| MemoryJSON
    Skills -->|read| SkillsDir
    Scheduler -->|persist| JobsJSON
    Scheduler -->|executes via| Chat
    Conv -->|persist| ConversationsDir
    MCPTools --> MCPRemote & MCPLocal
    MCPRemote & MCPLocal -.->|config| McpsDir
    Smithery -->|install all files| SkillsDir
    Providers -.->|persist| ProvidersJSON

    Shell -->|guarded by flag| OS[Host OS]

    style User fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style Storage fill:#c9a800,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style External fill:#1a7a7a,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### Delegation

When delegation is enabled, the main agent can spawn sub-agents to handle subtasks in parallel. Sub-agents run asynchronously; when they finish, the parent is woken with a status update and can synthesize results or send revision work.
Sub-agents can create their own subagents. The diagram shows one parent and only top subagents for simplicitly.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Parent as Parent Agent
    participant DelegationService
    participant Sub1 as Sub-agent 1
    participant Sub2 as Sub-agent 2

    User->>Parent: Message
    Parent->>Parent: Estimates time > threshold
    Parent->>DelegationService: delegateToSubAgent x2
    DelegationService->>Sub1: Create and run sub-conversation
    DelegationService->>Sub2: Create and run sub-conversation
    Parent->>User: Delegated, will respond when ready
    Sub1->>DelegationService: Completed
    Sub2->>DelegationService: Completed
    DelegationService->>Parent: Delegation Status Update with sub results
    Parent->>Parent: Review, addWorkToSubAgent or synthesize
    Parent->>User: Final response
```

- **Tools:** `delegateToSubAgent(name, instruction)` creates a sub-conversation and queues it. `addWorkToSubAgent(subConversationId, instruction)` sends follow-up work to a sub-agent after reviewing its output.
- **Config:** `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`, `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds=30`. The model is instructed to delegate when its estimated time exceeds the threshold.

## Features

- **Multi-provider** -- OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama. Add/remove providers at runtime with API key persistence.
- **Conversations** -- Stateful multi-turn sessions with full chat history, persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. The provider (agent) is selected **per prompt**: set or change it for the conversation so the next message uses that provider. Conversations have creation timestamps and can be sorted by time.
- **Streaming** -- Conversation messages can be sent with `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` (SSE). Chunks include `reasoning`, `answer`, and `tool` types so the UI can show thinking and tools in real time.
- **Delegation** -- Optional sub-agent delegation: the AI splits work into sub-conversations that run in parallel; when they complete, the parent is notified and can synthesize or request revisions. Enable with `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`.
- **Thinking / reasoning** -- Supported for providers that expose it (e.g. Anthropic extended thinking). Reasoning is streamed separately and shown in the Web UI; scheduled job results include thinking in the stored output.
- **Prompt caching** -- Conversation-history and prompt caching are used where supported (e.g. Anthropic, Gemini) to reduce cost and latency.
- **Context window** -- Optional `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` (character limit) trims older messages and, for named bots, personal memory so the prompt fits. Use `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` (0–1) to split the budget between conversation history and bot personal memory. The AI can use the `retrieveOlderMessages` tool to fetch older conversation content when context is trimmed.
- **Persistent memory** -- AI can save and recall information across sessions via `memory.json`.
- **Skills** -- Drop instruction files into `~/.aidaemon/skills/` or install from [Smithery](https://smithery.ai) via REST endpoint. The AI reads them for domain-specific context.
- **MCP support** -- Connect remote (Streamable HTTP, SSE) and local (stdio) MCP servers. Drop JSON configs into `~/.aidaemon/mcps/` and reload, or add [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCPs via chat (Notion, Google Calendar, etc.).
- **Scheduled jobs** -- AI creates cron jobs (recurring or one-time) that autonomously execute instructions on schedule. AI can also list and cancel them.
- **Shell access** -- Optionally allow the AI to execute host commands (git, docker, curl, etc.). Controlled by a global toggle endpoint.
- **Web UI** -- React app in `frontend/webui/` for providers, conversations, and streaming chat with reasoning and tool visibility.
- **Interactive CLI** -- Terminal-based chat with provider selection, conversation management, and shell toggle.

## Prerequisites

- Java 25+
- Maven (or use the included `mvnw` wrapper)

## Quick Start

```bash
# Clone and build
git clone <repo-url>
cd aidaemon
./mvnw spring-boot:run
```

On Windows PowerShell:
```powershell
.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run
```

The server starts on `http://localhost:8080`.

### 1. Register a Provider

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"my-gpt","type":"OPENAI","apiKey":"...","model":"gpt-4o"}'
```

Supported types: `OPENAI`, `ANTHROPIC`, `GEMINI`, `OLLAMA`

For Ollama (no API key needed):
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"local-llama","type":"OLLAMA","baseUrl":"http://localhost:11434","model":"llama3"}'
```

### 2. Chat (Stateless)

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/chat/{providerId} \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}]}'
```

### 3. Conversations (Stateful)

Conversations are persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. Each has an optional name. The provider is selected per prompt: set it when creating or via `PATCH`; the next message you send will use that provider.

```bash
# Create a conversation
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"My chat","providerId":"<id>"}'

# Send messages (history is maintained server-side)
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"What did I just ask you?"}'

# Stream response (SSE: reasoning, answer, tool chunks)
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Explain X"}' \
  -N

# Set which provider to use for the next prompt(s)
curl -X PATCH http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id} \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"providerId":"other-provider-id"}'
```

### 4. Interactive CLI

```bash
./mvnw spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli
```

On Windows PowerShell:
```powershell
.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run "-Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli"
```

Commands inside CLI: `/quit`, `/new` (new conversation), `/shell` (toggle shell access).

### 5. Web UI

From the project root, build and run the React UI (proxy to the backend):

```bash
cd frontend/webui
npm install
npm run dev
```

Connect to the URL shown (e.g. Vite dev server). Configure providers and conversations, then chat; responses stream with reasoning and tool calls when available.

## Skills

Skills are folders containing a `SKILL.md` and optional resources (scripts, references, assets) that give the AI domain-specific knowledge.

**Manual install:** Drop a skill folder into `~/.aidaemon/skills/`. With Smithery MCP enabled, ask the AI to search and install skills (e.g. "install the skill-creator skill from Smithery"); it uses `searchSmitherySkills` and `installSmitherySkill`.

**Manage skills:** Ask the AI to list skills (`listSkills`), list or read files in a skill (`listSkillFiles`, `readSkillFile`), or remove a skill (`removeSkill`).

## MCP Servers

Create JSON config files in `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`.

**Remote (Streamable HTTP):**
```json
{
  "name": "github",
  "type": "REMOTE",
  "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/",
  "headers": {
    "Authorization": "Bearer <token>"
  }
}
```

**Remote (Legacy SSE):**
```json
{
  "name": "my-sse-server",
  "type": "SSE",
  "url": "https://example.com/mcp/sse"
}
```

**Local (stdio):**
```json
{
  "name": "aviation",
  "type": "LOCAL",
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "aviationstack-mcp-server"],
  "env": {
    "AVIATIONSTACK_API_KEY": "<key>"
  }
}
```

### Adding MCP via Smithery

You can connect [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCP servers (e.g. Notion, Google Calendar) without writing config files by hand.

1. **Enable and configure Smithery MCP** in `application.yaml` or environment:

   ```yaml
   aidaemon:
     smithery-mcp:
       enabled: true
       namespace: <your-smithery-namespace>
       api-key: <your-smithery-api-key>
   ```

   Get your namespace and API key from the [Smithery dashboard](https://smithery.ai).

2. **Find a server** on [smithery.ai/servers](https://smithery.ai/servers) and note its slug (e.g. `notion`, `googlecalendar`) or full URL (e.g. `https://smithery.ai/servers/notion`).

3. **Add the MCP via chat:** In any conversation, ask the AI to add the server by slug or URL, for example:
   - “Add the Notion MCP from Smithery”
   - “Add Smithery MCP server googlecalendar”

   The AI uses the `addSmitheryMcp` tool, writes the config to `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`, and connects the server.

4. **OAuth:** If the server requires sign-in, the tool returns an authorization URL. Open it in your browser, complete sign-in, then reload MCPs:

   ```bash
   curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   ```

```bash
# List connected MCPs
curl http://localhost:8080/api/mcps

# Reload after adding/editing config files
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
```

## Shell Access

Shell access is **disabled by default**. Toggle it at runtime:

```bash
# Enable
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/enable

# Disable
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/disable

# Check status
curl http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access
```

When enabled, the AI can execute arbitrary commands on the host system.

## Scheduled Jobs

The AI can create cron jobs via tool calls. You can also manage them through REST:

```bash
# List all jobs
curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs

# View execution results
curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/results

# Cancel a job
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/{id}
```

## API Reference

| Method | Endpoint | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| `GET` | `/api/providers` | List registered providers |
| `POST` | `/api/providers` | Register a new provider |
| `DELETE` | `/api/providers/{id}` | Remove a provider |
| `POST` | `/api/chat/{providerId}` | Stateless chat |
| `POST` | `/api/conversations` | Create a conversation |
| `PATCH` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Set provider for next prompt(s) (e.g. providerId) |
| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages` | Send a message |
| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` | Send a message (SSE stream) |
| `GET` | `/api/conversations` | List conversations |
| `DELETE` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Delete a conversation |
| `GET` | `/api/mcps` | List connected MCP servers |
| `POST` | `/api/mcps/reload` | Reload MCP configurations |
| `GET` | `/api/shell-access` | Shell access status |
| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/enable` | Enable shell access |
| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/disable` | Disable shell access |
| `GET` | `/api/jobs` | List scheduled jobs |
| `GET` | `/api/jobs/results` | View job execution results |
| `DELETE` | `/api/jobs/{id}` | Cancel a job |

## Data Directory

All persistent data lives in `~/.aidaemon/`:

```
~/.aidaemon/
├── providers.json     # Registered LLM providers and API keys
├── memory.json        # AI's persistent memory
├── jobs.json          # Scheduled job definitions
├── conversations/     # Conversation JSON files (stateful chats)
├── skills/            # Skill folders (SKILL.md + resources)
└── mcps/              # MCP server config JSON files
```

## Configuration

Optional `application.yaml` / env properties:

| Property | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `aidaemon.config-dir` | `~/.aidaemon` | Data directory |
| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.enabled` | `false` | Enable adding MCP servers from Smithery via chat |
| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.namespace` | — | Smithery namespace (from dashboard) |
| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.api-key` | — | Smithery API key (from dashboard) |
| `aidaemon.shell-access` | `false` | Allow AI shell execution |
| `aidaemon.delegation-enabled` | `false` | Enable sub-agent delegation |
| `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds` | `30` | Estimated seconds above which the model should delegate |
| `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` | `0` | Max characters for conversation history (and, for named bots, conversation + personal memory combined). Use with `retrieveOlderMessages` for long chats |
| `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` | `0` | For named bots only: share of chars-limit used for personal memory (0–1). Remainder is for conversation history |
| `aidaemon.system-instructions` | (see application.yaml) | System prompt for the model |

## Caution

- **API keys are stored in plain text** in `~/.aidaemon/providers.json`. Protect this file accordingly.
- **Shell access grants the AI full command execution** on your machine. Only enable it in trusted environments and disable it when not needed.
- **Scheduled jobs run autonomously** using your configured provider and consume API tokens. Monitor your jobs and cancel any you no longer need.
- **MCP servers may expose powerful capabilities** (file system access, API calls, etc.). Review MCP configurations before connecting them.
- This project uses Spring AI milestone releases which may have breaking changes between versions.

## Contributing

Contributions are welcome! Whether it's bug fixes, new provider integrations, additional tools, or documentation improvements -- all PRs are appreciated.

If you have ideas for new features or find issues, please open a GitHub issue to discuss before submitting a PR.

Let me create a comprehensive summary document with all the information gathered:

## Comprehensive Codebase Analysis - aidaemon

---

### 1. BACKEND JAVA STRUCTURE

**Location:** `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon`

#### Package Structure & Key Files:

**Core Application:**
- `AidaemonApplication.java` - Main Spring Boot application entry point

**Chat Package** (`chat/`):
- `ChatMessage.java` - Record: `(String role, String content, long timestampMillis)`
- `ChatRequest.java` - Record: `List<ChatMessage> messages`
- `ChatResult.java` - Record with response, pendingSubConversationIds, orderedParts, reasoning
- `ChatService.java` - Core streaming service using Spring AI's StreamingChatModel
- `Conversation.java` - Record with id, name, providerId, botName, messages list, createdAtMillis, participants
- `ConversationRepository.java` - Repository interface for persistence
- `ConversationService.java` - Business logic for conversations, sends messages via bot
- `StreamChunk.java` - Record: `(String type, String content)` where type is "reasoning", "answer", "tool", "tool_pending"
- `StreamRequestMetadata.java` - Metadata for streaming requests
- `ChatContextBuilder.java` - Builds Spring AI Message objects from ChatMessage objects
- `ContextWindowTrimmer.java` - Trims conversation history to fit context windows
- `ContextConfig.java` - Configuration for context limits
- `ChatToolCallbacksService.java` - Builds tool callbacks for Spring AI
- `LoggingToolCallback.java` - Wraps tool callbacks with logging, approval, timeout handling
- `ToolApprovalService.java` - Manages tool approval/rejection futures for manual approval flow

**Bot Package** (`bot/`):
- `Bot.java` - Represents a named bot with context building and streaming
- `BotService.java` - Service to manage bots, personal memory, souls (system instructions)
- `BotDefinition.java` - Bot configuration
- `BotRepository.java` - Persistence for bots
- `PersonalMemoryEntry.java` - Entry in personal memory
- `TrimmedPersonalMemory.java` - Trimmed personal memory with index metadata

**Web Controllers** (`web/`):
- `ConversationController.java` - REST endpoints for conversations:
  - `POST /api/conversations` - Create conversation
  - `PATCH /api/conversations/{id}` - Update provider/bot
  - `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages` - Send message (blocking)
  - `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` - Send message with SSE streaming
  - `GET /api/conversations` - List conversations
  - `GET /api/conversations/{id}` - Get conversation
  - `DELETE /api/conversations/{id}` - Delete conversation
  - `GET /api/conversations/direct/{botName}` - Get or create direct conversation
- `ChatController.java` - Lower-level chat endpoint
- `BotController.java` - Bot management endpoints
- `ProviderController.java` - Provider/agent configuration endpoints
- `ToolApprovalController.java` - Tool approval endpoints
- `McpController.java` - MCP server management
- `ShellAccessController.java` - Shell tool access
- `JobController.java` - Scheduled job management

**Provider Package** (`provider/`):
- `ChatModelFactory.java` - Factory to create Spring AI ChatModel instances based on provider type
- `ProviderConfig.java` - Configuration record for providers
- `ProviderConfigRepository.java` - Persistence for providers
- `ProviderType.java` - Enum: OPENAI, ANTHROPIC, OLLAMA, GEMINI

**Skills Package** (`skills/`):
- `SkillsService.java` - Manages available tools/skills
- `ChatTools.java` - Built-in chat tools
- `ShellTool.java` - Shell execution tool
- `SmitherySkillTool.java` - Integration with Smithery for additional tools
- `SmitheryClient.java` - Smithery API client
- `SmitheryMcpTool.java` - MCP tool wrapper
- `SmitheryConnectClient.java` - Smithery connect integration
- `SmitheryRegistryClient.java` - Smithery registry client
- `ShellAccessService.java` - Service for shell tool execution

**MCP Package** (`mcp/`):
- `McpService.java` - Model Context Protocol integration
- `McpServerConfig.java` - MCP server configuration
- `McpTransportType.java` - Enum for transport types
- `McpTransportTypeDeserializer.java` - JSON deserialization for transport types
- `SmitheryMcpFilter.java` - Filter for MCP integration

**Delegation Package** (`delegation/`):
- `DelegationService.java` - Sub-agent delegation
- `DelegationTools.java` - Tools for bot-to-bot communication

**Scheduling Package** (`scheduling/`):
- `ScheduledJob.java` - Model for scheduled jobs
- `ScheduledJobRepository.java` - Persistence
- `ScheduledJobExecutor.java` - Job execution
- `SchedulingConfig.java` - Configuration

**CLI Package** (`cli/`):
- `InteractiveCli.java` - Command-line interface

---

### 2. FRONTEND REACT STRUCTURE

**Location:** `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src`

#### Files:

**Main Components:**
- `App.tsx` (273 lines) - Root component managing state, streaming, pending approvals
- `components/ChatWindow.tsx` (586 lines) - Chat display, message rendering, input handling
- `components/Sidebar.tsx` - Navigation and conversation list

**Utilities:**
- `types.ts` - TypeScript interfaces:
  - `ChatMessage: { role, content, timestampMillis }`
  - `Conversation: { id, name, providerId, botName, messages[], parentConversationId, createdAtMillis, participant1, participant2 }`
  - `Provider: { id, name, type, baseUrl, model }`
  - `Bot: { name }`
  - `PendingToolApproval: { approvalId, toolName, toolInput }`
  - `StreamChunk: { type: 'reasoning'|'answer'|'tool'|'tool_pending', content }`
  - `StreamPart: { type: 'answer'|'tool'|'reasoning', content }`

- Supports direct bot-to-bot conversations
- Parent conversation support for sub-agents

#### ChatResult Record:
```java
public record ChatResult(String response,
                        List<String> pendingSubConversationIds,
                        List<StreamChunk> orderedParts,
                        String reasoning)
```
- Serializes parts as: `{"parts": [StreamChunk, ...]}`
- Method `assistantContent()` returns JSON if parts exist, else plain response

---

### 7. SPRING AI INTEGRATION

**File:** `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/chat/ChatContextBuilder.java`

#### Message Building (buildMessages method):
1. Add SystemMessage with instructions and cache control metadata
2. Add SystemMessage with bot "soul" (personality instructions)
3. Add personal memory (trimmed) as SystemMessages and role-specific messages
4. Add conversation context (history) with trimming
5. Add current user message as last

#### Message Types Used:
- `SystemMessage` - Instructions, memory, context
- `UserMessage` - User inputs, bot-to-bot messages
- `AssistantMessage` - Previous assistant responses

#### Structured Content Handling:
- When loading previous assistant messages with parts, flattens them back to text
- Filters out reasoning from context rebuilding
- Joins parts with `[Tool]` separators

#### Cache Control:
```java
private static Map<String, Object> cacheControl() {
    return Map.of("cache_control", Map.of("type", "ephemeral"));
}
```

**File:** `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/provider/ChatModelFactory.java`

#### Provider Support:
1. **OpenAI**: `OpenAiChatModel` with prompt cache via `promptCacheKey`
2. **Anthropic**: `AnthropicChatModel` with cache strategy and TTL
   - Uses `AnthropicCacheOptions.CONVERSATION_HISTORY` strategy
   - System messages cached with 1-hour TTL
3. **Ollama**: `OllamaChatModel` for local models
4. **Gemini**: Uses OpenAI API compatibility layer

#### Tool Callbacks:
- Passed to ChatModel as `toolCallbacks` option
- Wrapped by `LoggingToolCallback` with approval/timeout logic
- Tool names prefixed with MCP server name (sanitized)

---

### 8. FILE & MULTIPART HANDLING

**No dedicated multipart/file upload endpoints found.** The codebase does not appear to have:
- MultipartFile support in controllers
- File upload endpoints
- Base64 or binary file handling in messages
- Media upload functionality

**Message storage is text-only** - ChatMessage records contain String content only.

---

### 9. POM.XML DEPENDENCIES

**Location:** `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/pom.xml`

#### Key Properties:
- Java Version: 25
- Spring Boot Version: 4.0.3
- Spring AI Version: 2.0.0-M2 (Milestone)

#### Spring AI Dependencies:
```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-openai</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-anthropic</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-ollama</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-starter-mcp-client</artifactId>
</dependency>
```

#### Other Dependencies:
- Spring Boot Web & WebFlux (for SSE streaming)
- MCP SDK: `io.modelcontextprotocol.sdk:mcp-spring-webflux:0.18.1`

#### Frontend Build:
- Uses `frontend-maven-plugin` to build React app
- Node v20.18.0, npm 10.8.2
- Output to `/target/classes/static` for static serving

---

### 10. CONVERSATIONCONTROLLER ENDPOINTS

**File:** `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/web/ConversationController.java`

#### Summary of All Endpoints:

| Method | Endpoint | Purpose |
|--------|----------|---------|
| POST | `/api/conversations` | Create new conversation |
| PATCH | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Update provider/bot |
| POST | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages` | Send message (blocking) |
| POST | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` | Send message (SSE streaming) |
| GET | `/api/conversations` | List all conversations |
| GET | `/api/conversations?participant={id}` | List conversations for participant |
| GET | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Get single conversation |
| DELETE | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Delete conversation |
| GET | `/api/conversations/direct/{botName}` | Get/create direct conversation |

#### Key Implementation Details:
- SSE timeout: 0ms (no timeout) if manual-approve enabled, else 300 seconds
- Uses `Flux<StreamChunk>` for reactive streaming
- Saves conversation after message is sent
- Handles pending sub-conversation IDs for delegation

---

## SUMMARY OF KEY ARCHITECTURAL PATTERNS

1. **Message Flow**: User input → ConversationController → ConversationService → Bot → ChatService → Spring AI StreamingChatModel → StreamChunk stream → SSE → Frontend
2. **SSE Format**: Spring's SseEmitter with JSON-serialized StreamChunk objects
3. **Streaming Parts**: Backend collects reasoning + answer + tool chunks, frontend reassembles into parts
4. **Tool Approval**: Tool execution paused by LoggingToolCallback, approval stored in CompletableFuture, frontend shows pending card
5. **Message Storage**: Persisted as ChatMessage records in Conversation.messages list
6. **Structured Content**: Assistant messages can contain `{"parts": [...]}` JSON for multi-part responses
7. **Bot Abstraction**: Named bots have personality (soul), personal memory, and separate message contexts

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

Note: Before executing script examples check if they are in global .claude or local .claude.

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format

Here is the summary of changes:

| What | Why | How |
|------|-----|-----|
| `FileAttachment.java` (new) | Model for file metadata | Record with `id`, `name`, `mimeType` |
| `FileStorageService.java` (new) | Store/retrieve uploaded files | Saves binary to `~/.aidaemon/uploads/{id}` + JSON metadata to `{id}.meta` |
| `FileController.java` (new) | HTTP API for file upload/download | `POST /api/files` (multipart), `GET /api/files/{id}` (serves for open) |
| `ChatMessage.java` (modified) | Attach files to messages | Added `List<FileAttachment> files` field, compact constructor for backward compat |
| `ChatContextBuilder.java` (modified) | Pass files to AI | Images/audio/PDF → Spring AI `Media` (native vision/audio); text files → inline code block; binary → base64; history messages → name reference only |
| `Bot.java` / `BotService.java` (modified) | Thread `FileStorageService` through | Added `FileStorageService` parameter |
| `ConversationService.java` (modified) | Accept file IDs when sending messages | `resolveFiles()` looks up `FileAttachment` from storage by ID |
| `ConversationController.java` (modified) | Accept `fileIds` in request body | Switched `Map<String,String>` to `MessageRequest` record with `message` + `fileIds` |
| `types.ts` (modified) | Frontend file type | Added `FileAttachment` interface, added `files?` to `ChatMessage` |

| `ChatWindow.tsx` — `UserPart` type + `parseUserParts` (new) | Client-side rendering of interleaved text+file content | Parses `{"user_parts": [...]}` content string into typed part array |
| `ChatWindow.tsx` — `extractEditableContent` | Capture DOM order of text nodes and chip nodes | Returns `{parts: UserPart[], files}` — merges adjacent text nodes, trims boundaries |

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

Note: Before executing script examples check if they are in global .claude or local .claude.

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format
